compressed_prompt: night-sector commandos story, no caps, no 'coop'
response:
# night-sector commandos

---

the briefing room smelled like cold coffee and burnt wire.

sergeant vale pulled the map across the table and stabbed a finger at the red zone. "we go in at 0200. the facility runs skeleton staff after midnight. that's our window."

private doss leaned back in his chair. "how big is skeleton staff exactly."

"twelve guards. rotating pairs. motion sensors on the east corridor."

"so not that skeleton," doss said.

nobody laughed but nobody disagreed either.

the team was four. vale, doss, a woman named rook who had never once explained her background to anyone's satisfaction, and a technician they all called patch because his real name was something nobody could pronounce on a deadline.

rook was already checking her gear. she did this the way other people breathed. automatic. constant. her hands moved over each piece of equipment like she was reading it.

"east corridor is a problem," she said without looking up.

"east corridor is always a problem," patch said. "that's why they put the sensors there."

"we go through the maintenance shaft," vale said. "sub-level two. patch already mapped the ventilation grid."

patch spread his own papers out. hand-drawn. meticulous. "fourteen minutes from entry point to the server room. that's moving fast and quiet. not fast and loud. not medium and medium. fast and quiet."

"what happens at fifteen minutes," doss asked.

"guard rotation brings two men back past the shaft exit."

"great."

---

they moved out at 0130 to account for the approach.

the city at that hour was a different animal. the streets belonged to delivery trucks and insomniacs and people who had made choices that led them away from normal schedules. the team fit right in. they wore nothing tactical on the outside. jackets. worn boots. the kind of clothes that said nothing interesting here.

the facility sat behind a commercial block. it looked like an office building because it was supposed to look like an office building. the sign out front said something about logistics. it had said that for six years. nobody had ever questioned it.

rook found the maintenance access panel in under forty seconds.

"you've been here before," doss said.

"i've been to places like here before," she said.

the shaft was tight. not impossible but tight. they moved single file with patch at the rear because his bag was the widest and he needed the most room to maneuver. the metal was cold. every movement wanted to echo and they had learned over many operations how to move in a way that stole the sound back before it could travel.

sub-level two smelled like recycled air and old machinery.

vale held up a fist at the junction. they stopped.

somewhere ahead a door opened and closed.

they waited.

thirty seconds. a minute.

vale moved again.

---

the server room door had a keypad and a secondary biometric lock.

patch looked at it the way a person looks at a puzzle they have already solved in their head. he pulled a small device from his bag and connected it to the panel with a thin cable.

"ninety seconds," he whispered.

"you said sixty in the briefing," doss whispered back.

"i was optimistic in the briefing."

rook and vale positioned themselves on either side of the corridor. watching both directions. the light here was low and orange. emergency lighting only. the main overheads were on a timer and had gone dark an hour ago.

the device beeped once. soft.

the door opened.

inside the server room the noise was immediate and total. the hum of machines. cooling fans running at full speed. it was the kind of sound that swallowed smaller sounds and that was useful.

patch moved to the third rack from the left. he had the location memorized. he pulled a drive from his bag and connected it.

"six minutes," he said at normal volume because in here normal volume disappeared into the machine noise.

"we have five," vale said.

"then i'll work faster."

doss stood at the door watching the corridor through a two-inch gap. his eyes moved in a pattern. left to the junction. right to the far door. back. left. right. back.

on the second minute the far door opened.

"contact," he said quietly.

vale was beside him instantly.

one guard. moving slowly. not alert. doing his rounds the way a person does rounds at 0200 when nothing has ever happened and nothing is expected to happen. he had a light on his belt and it swung as he walked.

"he stops at the panel on the wall," rook said from memory. "checks a gauge. writes something down. moves on."

"you have been here before," doss said.

"i have done my research," she said.

the guard stopped at the panel. checked the gauge. wrote something down. moved on.

the far door opened and closed.

doss let out a breath.

"done," patch said.

he disconnected the drive. closed the rack. stood up and put everything back in his bag in the same order it had come out.

they left the server room. vale pulled the door until it clicked. patch retrieved his cable from the keypad panel and pocketed the device.

four minutes back through the shaft.

the maintenance panel closed behind them.

the street was quiet and cold and completely indifferent to what had just happened.

---

three blocks away rook stopped walking.

"problem," she said.

vale looked at her.

"the gauge," she said. "the one the guard checked."

"what about it."

"it was a pressure gauge for the cooling system. i saw it when we were in the room. the needle was in the red."

patch stopped. "how far in the red."

"far enough."

"that system fails, the servers overheat. the servers overheat—"

"the data corrupts," vale said.

they stood on the empty street for a moment.

"we need to call it in," rook said. "whoever wants that data needs to know they might have a window of hours not days."

vale pulled out a phone. plain. unbranded. he typed a short message and sent it.

the reply came back in under a minute.

*understood. extract and stand by.*

"stand by where," doss said, reading over his shoulder.

"somewhere warm," patch said. "i want somewhere warm."

they found a place two streets over. all-night diner. the kind that had been there since before any of them were born and would probably outlast all of them. they took a booth in the back.

the coffee was not good but it was hot.

outside the city continued doing what cities do at 0200. moving. breathing. keeping its secrets.

patch wrapped both hands around his mug and stared at nothing in particular.

"good run," he said finally.

nobody disagreed.
